critic_raw:
The robustness claim to spatial augmentations (shifting and rotation) is not a true generalization test if the reference image is learned on training data that includes those same augmentations. In that case, the model has been directly trained to handle the claimed perturbations, and 'robustness' is merely in-distribution performance rather than a generalization property.

The 'small amount of data' for reference learning is left unquantified. Phase retrieval algorithms are highly sensitive to the ratio of measurements to unknowns (the oversampling ratio). Without specifying training set size relative to signal dimensionality, the efficiency claim is unverifiable and comparisons to classical algorithms are not on equal footing.

The number of unrolling iterations is a critical architectural hyperparameter in unrolled networks — it determines effective model depth and computational budget. Varying the number of iterations changes the model substantially, yet this parameter is not reported. Different iteration counts would produce different reconstruction quality / speed trade-offs that could confound comparisons.

The generalization to 'unseen data distributions' is claimed but the specific out-of-distribution test is not described. Phase retrieval difficulty depends strongly on signal sparsity and the measurement operator. If the unseen distribution has similar sparsity and operator structure to training data, this is near-in-distribution evaluation that does not support a broad generalization claim.
all_issues_raised: The robustness to spatial augmentations (shifting, rotation) is not a true generalization test if the reference was learned on data containing those same augmentations — this is in-distribution performance, not generalization.
Training set size relative to signal dimensionality is unspecified, making the 'small amount of data' claim unverifiable and comparisons to classical algorithms unfair without normalization.
The number of unrolling iterations — a critical architectural parameter determining model depth and capacity — is not reported, preventing reproduction or fair comparison.

The 'unseen data distribution' generalization test is unspecified; if the test distribution shares sparsity structure and measurement operator with training data, this is near-in-distribution evaluation.
all_issues_adjudicated: The robustness to spatial augmentations (shifting, rotation) is not a true generalization test if the reference was learned on data containing those same augmentations — this is in-distribution performance, not generalization.
Training set size relative to signal dimensionality is unspecified, making the 'small amount of data' claim unverifiable and comparisons to classical algorithms unfair without normalization.
The number of unrolling iterations — a critical architectural parameter determining model depth and capacity — is not reported, preventing reproduction or fair comparison.

The 'unseen data distribution' generalization test is unspecified; if the test distribution shares sparsity structure and measurement operator with training data, this is near-in-distribution evaluation.
